story:
I’m tapping my foot on the marble, watching the elevator numbers drop, already late on a week when I swapped hours so George could pick up his kids while his wife recovered from surgery. Starting at ten should make me early, but I tried adding a morning gym session. Shower and arrive late, or skip it and arrive sweaty? I choose late. The doors open; she appears in a rush of heels and curls, juggling a satchel and briefcase. Floor 23 for her, 22 for me. I don’t speak, but in my head I do. I name her Eleanor, like the song. I decide she’s with Jackson & Fuller on 23, maybe an intern, maybe six years in—no, too young—yes, an intern. I imagine us talking about our jobs, about how I’m an assistant accountant on 22. I picture her nodding to music pulsing in her AirPods. I ask what she’s listening to—Miles Davis, she’d say. Kind of Blue. My favorite. The elevator dings; I step out, still silent.

Tuesday, I arrive early, lingering in the foyer, hoping. The click of heels, and there she is again, hair tamed, bags still bulging. I imagine saying “Hi” and her warm, throaty reply. I picture us talking about late starts making us later, about how we’re both actually better at mornings. I almost ask her to coffee, then remember I’ve never actually spoken to her. The doors open on my floor; I let the moment go.

Wednesday, I stall until the last second—no heels—and step into the empty elevator. Just as the doors close, the tap-tap-tap comes, urgent. I block the doors; she slips in, breathless.

“Thanks,” she whispers. Our first real word.

I imagine saying, Running late again? and her rueful grin. I imagine inviting her to The Duke, the jazz bar around the corner, a bottle of wine and live music. I check who’s playing on my phone; my floor arrives; I step out, and the doors close on any chance I had.

Thursday, I’m the late one. My cat, Mr. Whiskers, left a half-digested mouse by the door at the worst moment. I sprint from the bus; the last elevator is closing—her hand flashes out and saves it. I mumble something that isn’t quite English. In my head, I tell her about my feral-turned-spoiled cat. We swap cat stories in the world where I speak. I fall a little further in love. The elevator stops at 22; I get off.

Friday, my last late start. I’m so early I pace with a cappuccino, promising myself I’ll talk to her: coffee, lunch, The Duke, anything. The minutes slide past. She doesn’t come. She won’t; it’s her last day. She’s upstairs already, boxing her desk on 23, the gold plastic nameplate nestled on top. I ride up alone.

After a long day finishing files for George, I ride down, exhausted. The doors open—and she’s there, holding a box of her things. A gold plastic nameplate winks up: Tamara Blank. She gives me a small smile.

“It’s my last day,” she says.

The doors open on ground; she steps out; her heels tap away across the marble. I stay inside, staring at my reflection as the doors close.

Ah, look at all the lonely people.
